coredata.dc:title: Electrocatalytic performance of green synthesized nitrogen-doped carbon dots toward clean energy H2 generation
coredata.dc:creator: Selvaraju, Nithya
Murugan, Jaganathan
Selvaraj, Senthilnathan
Singhal, Neeraj
Venugopal, Gunasekaran
coredata.dc:description: Non-metal carbonaceous and elemental doping are efficient methods for improving the electrocatalytic activity of the hydrogen evolution reaction (HER). Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots were produced under various conditions and operate exceptionally well in an acidic environment. The results of the experiments show that nitrogen doping improved hydrogen adsorption and accelerated HER kinetics. It demonstrates higher performance with an overpotential of −0.268 V due to doping, which indirectly lowered electron-hole pair recombination as evidenced by time-constant estimation, as well as outstanding stability. This research will provide a viable platinum-free material that may be used as an effective electrocatalyst for the HER reaction.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots
Electrocatalyst
Tafel slope
Hydrogen
Overpotential

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XPS spectra (a) Full survey Spectrum, Deconvoluted (b) C 1s (c) N 1s (d) O 1s spectra.
(a) LSV spectra and (b) Tafel slope of bare GC, n-CDs @different conditions (c) Chrono-potentiometry @ 10 mA/cm2 and (d) LSV spectra before and after 25 h.
Bode plots of (a) bare GC (b) n-CDs (Sample S3) coated (c) Nyquist plot of bare GC and S3 coated (d) Time constant studies (e) CV Curves at different scan rates (f) current .density Vs scan rate.

head.ce:title: Electrocatalytic performance of green synthesized nitrogen-doped carbon dots toward clean energy H2 generation

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Advanced Nanomaterials and System Lab, Department of Materials Science, Central University of Tamil Nadu, Thiruvarur 610 005, Tamil Nadu, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Advanced Nanomaterials and System Lab, Department of Materials Science, Central University of Tamil Nadu, Thiruvarur 610 005, Tamil Nadu, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Metallurgical and Materials Engineering, Indian Institute of Technology Ropar, Punjab, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Metallurgical and Materials Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Metallurgical and Materials Engineering, Indian Institute of Technology Ropar, Punjab, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, SSN College of Engineering, Kalavakkam, Chennai 603 110, Tamil Nadu, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physics, SSN College of Engineering, Kalavakkam, Chennai 603 110, Tamil Nadu, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Non-metal carbonaceous and elemental doping are efficient methods for improving the electrocatalytic activity of the hydrogen evolution reaction (HER). Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots were produced under various conditions and operate exceptionally well in an acidic environment. The results of the experiments show that nitrogen doping improved hydrogen adsorption and accelerated HER kinetics. It demonstrates higher performance with an overpotential of −0.268 V due to doping, which indirectly lowered electron-hole pair recombination as evidenced by time-constant estimation, as well as outstanding stability. This research will provide a viable platinum-free material that may be used as an effective electrocatalyst for the HER reaction.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In the contemporary world, hydrogen fuel is an eco-friendly energy carrier with high energy density [1]. Clean green hydrogen energy can be produced using electrocatalysis hydrogen evolution reaction, which is in desperate need of a catalyst with faster kinetics. Despite being more effective, using platinum as a catalyst is limited by its high price and scarcity [2]. Carbon-based materials are gaining popularity in H2 generation due to their superior endurance in acidic and alkaline media, which is aided by a larger electrochemical surface area with numerous active sites [3,4]. In particular nanosized carbon particles assist in maintaining the direction of electron transport in recombination reactions [5]. The magical carbon candidate is zero-dimensional carbon dots (CDs) which has the advantage of abundant anchoring and active sites because of rich in surface functional groups. Doping of heteroatom with CDs provides a significant change in the distribution of charge that nurtures the electron transfer through the interactions which could effectively enhance HER. Hence, in this study, nitrogen-doped carbon dots (n-CDs) has been chosen, and the same has been synthesized by hydrothermal method with citrus medica fruit juice under various conditions such as pH, temperature, and timings (5 h and 8 h) which has the advantage of low toxicity and is abundantly available.

Using cost-effective hydrothermal technique, the samples of n-CDs were prepared at two different timings (5 h, 8 h), three various temperatures (180 °C, 210 °C, 240 °C), and three different pH (1,7,14), and they are named as S2(pH7, 240 °C, 8 h), S3(pH14, 240 °C, 8 h), S4(pH14, 210 °C, 8 h), S5(pH7, 180 °C, 8 h), S6(pH7, 180 °C, 8 h), S7(pH7, 240 °C, 5 h), S8(pH, 180 °C, 5 h, undoped) for further reference. Ammonium hydroxide was added to modify the pH, and the same was used for doping heteroatoms (nitrogen). The detailed preparation of n-CDs, parameter settings, and working electrode is provided in our earlier work and also in supplementary section(SI) [6].

Linear sweep voltammetry (LSV), Impedance studies (EIS), and chrono-potentiometry (CP) tests were carried out in ambient temperature to determine the kinetic parameters like electric double layer capacitance (EDLC), electrochemically active surface area (ECSA) and stability, and results were expressed to reversible hydrogen electrode (RHE) using Nernst Equation.
(1)
E
v s
R H E
=
E
( v s
A g / A g C l )
+
E
A g / A g C l
r e f
+ 0.0591 V
×
p H
E
Ag / Agcl
r e f
= 0.1976
V

In supplementary, Fig. A1(a), the amorphous nature of n-CDs (Sample S3) has been revealed by broad XRD diffraction peak at 20.5˚ attributed to the (002) plane of carbon. Meanwhile, the same was confirmed by the absence of diffraction rings in selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern of n-CDs exhibited by HRTEM images. The formation of uniform and spherical shaped n-CDs and their histogram for average particle were shown in Fig. A1(b) & (c). FT-IR spectroscopy was performed which confirms the presence of O-H/N-H stretching vibrations, C≡C stretching and C=C stretching/N-H bending vibrations shown as Fig. A2 in the SI [7]. The elemental composition was studied by XPS and exhibited in Fig. 1 (a). Existence of C, N and O bands in a ratio of 78.9/1.2/19.9 calculated from the full survey spectra. The deconvoluted individual C 1s, N 1s and O 1s and their states were also shown Fig. 1(b), (c) and (d). The presence of functional groups (carboxyl, hydroxyl, amine, and carbonyl) in n-CDs was clearly revealed, which enhances the dispersion capability in water.

The electrocatalytic performance towards H2 production was investigated by LSV, Tafel, and EIS measurements were carried out at a scan rate of 10 mV/s. The LSV curves of bare glassy carbon (GC) and n-CDs prepared at different conditions (S2, S3, S4, S5, S6, S7, S8) were computed. Fig. 2 shows the LSV curves, electro-kinetic parameters such as onset potential, overpotential at −10 mA/cm2 , and Tafel slopes were determined. The graph reveals that out of seven different conditions, the sample S3 prepared at a condition of pH14, 240 °C, 8 h exhibits the lowest overpotential of − 0.268 V, which is comparably very low with respect to −0.727 V of bare GC and −0.578 V of undoped CDs (S8). Similarly, the onset potential of GC, S8, and S3 were −0.49 V, −0.46 V, and −0.13 V, respectively. This result confirms the better electrocatalytic activity of HER by the sample S3, and the same was assured by the Tafel slope. It is vital parameter which predicts the faster kinetics revealed by, the smaller slope. The calculated values are 82 mV/dec,161 mV/dec, and 242 mV/dec of S3, S8, and bare GC, and the value of 82 mV/dec falls within the range of 40–120 mV/dec, implying a Volmer Heyrovsky mechanism. To check the durability, the chronopotentiometry test was performed for 25 hrs at constant current density of −10 mA/cm2. It shows a negligible change and exhibits good stability. To scrutinize the coating effect, an impedance study is a vital tool, and it was studied at the open circuit potential of 0.775 VRHE.

The resistance, EDLC, ECSA, and time constant were calculated using EIS studies which are revealed in Fig. 3 . The phase angle Bode plot of bare GC electrode clearly conclude that the existence of one time constant and two time constant by S3 coated electrode therefore two different equivalent circuits were used for fitting which are shown in Fig. 3(a) and (b). In Fig. 3(c), the Nyquist plot evidenced the resistance behaviour, which is found as drastically reduced. With the help of fitting circuit, the total resistance of bare GC is calculated as 4.23 × 105 Ω/cm2 which is higher than that of the total resistance of n-CDs sample coated (S3 − 0.58 × 103 Ω/cm2 ,) which revealed the improved electrical conductivity of n-CDs. This is achieved due to the decrease in electron-hole recombination with a greater number of electron trapping sites which provides electrochemical active surface area resulting the acceleration of electron transfer. Heteroatom doping creates more defects which enhance the number of catalytic active sites for charge trapping. This can be proved by the electron life time estimation by the following formula.
(2)
τ
n = 1 / 2 π
f
max
where
τ n
is the electron life time and
f
max
frequency of peak obtained in the low- frequency region which belongs to the electron transfer process [8]. The electron life time was calculated for undoped carbon dots (S8) and n-CDs sample S3 as 1.3 ms and 28 ms shown in Fig. 3. The increased lifetime clearly proves the decrement of recombination and enhances the electron transfer between the electrode and electrolyte in turn increases the H2 generation in the way of Volmer- Heyrovsky mechanism.

The electrocatalytic performance is highly depends on the ECSA in terms of double layer capacitance. At various scan rates, CV studies were recorded and shown in Fig. 3(e) and (f). The calculated double layer capacitance is about 0.069 mF/cm2. The ECSA was calculated using ECSA = Cdl/Cs. The specific capacitance Cs is 0.040 mF/cm2 [9]. The obtained

ECSA of n-CDs (coated) prepared at the conditions of pH14, temperature 240 °C of timing 8 h is 1.7 cm2 which promotes the HER kinetics [10]. The pH14 condition indirectly says the doping more amount of nitrogen modifies the surrounding carbon atom’s electronic charge. That enhances the electronegativity and attracts more hydrogen proton towards the surface which improves the HER [11].

In summary, the electrocatalytic activity of n-CDs (prepared @ different condition) towards HER was tested. In that n-CDs prepared with pH14, at 240 °C, for 8 h shows better catalytic activity with an overpotential of −0.268 V achieved through Volmer-Heyrovsky mechanism. It exhibits the Tafel slope of 82 mV/dec an-d the impedance studies shows the resistance drastically reduced when compared to bare GC. This becomes possible due to the nitrogen doping proved by increase in time constant compatively with undoped CDs. Our studies profoundly show that n-CDs can be a promising material for hydrogen production in future.

Nithya Selvaraju: Conceptualization, Visualization, Formal analysis, Methodology, Software, Writing – original draft. Jaganathan Murugan: Data curation. Senthilnathan Selvaraj: Validation. Neeraj Singhal: Investigation. Gunasekaran Venugopal: Resources, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are thankful to the Central University of Tamil Nadu, for providing a necessary research facility for this project.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.matlet.2023.134255.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article: XRD Pattern (A1), HRTEM image (A1), FTIR spectra (A2), comparison table and Electrode preparation were reported
Supplementary data 1
